exemplar.title:
How significant was the concealment of 
Leninâs testament in Stalinâs rise to power?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How significant was the concealment of 
Leninâs testament in Stalinâs rise to power?

strengths: The topic is communicated accurately and effectively, with every section directly reinforcing the investigation of Leninâs Testament.
The research question is concise, sharply focused and consistently addressed throughout the essay.
Section headings and structure maintain coherence and guide the reader clearly through the argument.
weaknesses: Minor typographical errors occasionally interrupt the flow of the text.
The methodology rationale could be strengthened by specifying particular archives or analytical frameworks.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both topic communication and research question clarity satisfy the top band descriptors without digression, meriting full marks. Methodology is complete and informed, despite a few webâsourced references; overall this criterion consistently aligns with the 5â6 band, warranting the maximum 6 points.

strengths: Subject-specific terminology (e.g., Politburo, cult of Lenin) is used accurately and consistently.
Knowledge of party structures and historiographical debates is clear and coherently integrated.
Source material is mostly relevant and effectively woven into the narrative.
weaknesses: Primary excerpts are seldom interrogated for provenance or bias.
A small number of non-peer-reviewed web sources weaken the academic rigour.
Occasional factual or spelling slips (e.g., âTroskyâ) distract from overall precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Application of source material and subject knowledge fall securely in the upper half of the 3â4 or 5â6 bands, but occasional lack of critical evaluation and minor inaccuracies prevent a perfect 6. Consistency of terminology and depth of understanding support a score of 5.

strengths: Research draws on a wide range of primary documents and leading secondary monographs, demonstrating strong source selection.
Argument is multi-faceted, weighing Leninâs Testament against structural and personal factors.
The conclusion synthesises findings and links back to historiographical debates.
weaknesses: Analysis depth is uneven, with some descriptive passages that lack rigorous causal evaluation.
Discussion of source bias and counterfactual scenarios remains underdeveloped.
Critical evaluation of historiansâ perspectives could be deepened for greater scholarly rigour.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality clearly occupies the 7â9 band, while analysis and evaluation lie in the 4â6 band. The strong breadth and relevance of evidence justify placing the criterion into the lowest mark of the 7â9 band, hence 7 points to reflect robust but not uniformly exceptional critical thinking.

strengths: All required IB elements (title page, TOC, sections, bibliography) are present and logically organised.
Font, spacing and heading hierarchy are consistent, aiding readability.
Sections and subheadings align exactly with the table of contents.
weaknesses: Page numbers are absent from the table of contents entries.
Minor citation inconsistencies appear in web references.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure fully meets top-band requirements, but small formatting lapses in footnotes and TOC prevent a perfect score. The overall presentation is clearly appropriate and coherent, meriting 3 out of 4 points.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Font, spacing and heading hierarchy are consistent, footnotes follow a recognisable style, and word count is within limits.  Pages appear to be numbered in the PDF extraction, satisfying formal requirements.

Small issuesâoccasional mis-formatted footnote markers (<sup><sup>)> and minor citation inconsistenciesâprevent a flawless presentation, but overall layout considerations are âpresent and correctly applied throughout.â  A score of 3 (mid-band) accurately reflects this competence.

The argument is logically organised: each main section addresses a factor, and the evaluation section explicitly balances short- and long-term significance before delivering a clear, supported judgement.  Multiple historiansâ interpretations are cited and weighed, showing some critical evaluation.

However, evaluation of source reliability and alternative explanations is often brief.  The conclusion, while reasoned, rests largely on previously stated points rather than fresh synthesis.  These features indicate âreasoned argumentâ¦with partial critical evaluation,â exactly matching the 4-6 descriptor.

Given the generally coherent structure but moderate depth of critique, 5 marks are appropriate.

The student identifies a sharply-defined historical issue â the concealment of Leninâs Testament â and immediately situates it within the broader context of Stalinâs succession struggle.  The title page, introduction and table of contents all reiterate the same focused topic, and every subsequent section heading relates explicitly to that theme.  There is no digression into unrelated areas of Soviet history, so the reader is in no doubt about what is being investigated.

Moreover, the topic is unquestionably suitable for a History EE: it concerns events that are more than ten years old, lends itself to primary/secondary source work and invites historiographical debate.  The student therefore meets the descriptor of âcommunicated accurately and effectively,â meriting the top band.

Minor lapses in wording (occasional typographical errors) do not hinder clarity, so the full 6 points within the 5â6 band are justified.

The essay contains all required IB elements: title page with word count, table of contents, introduction, clearly headed body sections, conclusion and bibliography.  The internal organisation mirrors the table of contents, aiding navigation.

Transitions are generally smooth and paragraphing logical, demonstrating an âclearly appropriateâ structure.  Hence the work sits in the upper band and merits the full 4 points.

A wide range of primary sources (Stalinâs speeches, Leninâs Testament, contemporary journals) and reputable secondary monographs (Kotkin, Service, Fitzpatrick, Suny) is listed and employed.  The introduction explicitly explains the rationale for combining primary documents with modern scholarship, demonstrating informed selection.

Citation is generally consistent and footnoted, and the bibliography is subdivided into primary/secondary categories, indicating systematic method.  A few web sources of questionable academic weight (e.g., Counterfire, student revision site) dilute the overall rigour and suggest slightly uneven discrimination.

Given this minor weakness, the methodology remains âcomplete with appropriate rangeâ but not exemplary, so 5 points within the top band are appropriate.

The bibliography shows breadth: roughly twenty scholarly books/articles plus primary documents.  Most are reputable academic works, demonstrating purposeful selection tailored to the question.  Each major claim is footnoted, so the reader can trace evidence.

Nevertheless, inclusion of a few non-academic websites and the absence of archival sources narrow the depth slightly.  Research therefore meets all elements of the 7-9 band but does not attain the sustained excellence needed for 8 +  marks.

Accordingly, the lowest value in the 7-9 band, 7, has been assigned.

The student moves beyond narrative by weighing the testamentâs importance against structural factors like Stalinâs control of the Secretariat and the weaknesses of rivals.  Paragraphs often end with mini-conclusions linking evidence to the research question, and historiographical viewpoints are compared.

Yet some sections lapse into description (long recounting of funeral propaganda, biographical details) without fully analysing causation, and there is little sustained testing of counter-evidence.  The depth is therefore uneven â solid but not consistently rigorous.

This mixed quality aligns with the mid-band (4-6); the stronger analytical passages merit 5 marks.

Most cited material is directly relevant to the research question, and the essay weaves quotations or paraphrases from Leninâs Testament, Stalinâs speeches, and key historians into the narrative.  This shows conscious application rather than mere listing.

However, engagement with the sourcesâ provenance or reliability is limited; primary extracts are seldom interrogated for bias, and web articles are treated on par with peer-reviewed scholarship.  Consequently, source use is âmostlyâ but not consistently appropriate.

These characteristics match the 3â4 band; the stronger parts justify the upper value of 4.

The student displays solid, coherent knowledge of the intra-party power struggle, accurately explaining roles of Zinoviev, Kamenev, Trotsky, and the organisational significance of the Secretariat.  Explanations of âLenin Enrolmentâ, âSocialism in One Countryâ and âPermanent Revolutionâ demonstrate command of key concepts.

Analysis is interwoven with historiographical positions (traditional vs. revisionist), indicating real understanding rather than rote description.  Occasional factual slips are minor and do not undermine overall accuracy.

Thus, the work fits securely in the top band, though the absence of deeper archival critique prevents a perfect 6; a score of 5 is warranted.

The research question â âHow significant was the concealment of Leninâs testament in Stalinâs rise to power?â â is concise, focused and invites judgement on a single causal factor.  It clearly signals an evaluative task rather than a descriptive narrative.

Throughout the essay the student keeps returning to this question: each analytical section weighs the testamentâs concealment against other factors, and the conclusion explicitly answers the âhow significantâ element.  There is no evidence of the discussion straying onto unrelated thematic ground.

Because the question is both clear and consistently addressed, the work fulfils the descriptor for the 5â6 band at a high level; hence 6 marks are awarded.

Terms such as âPolitburoâ, âOrgburoâ, âcult of Leninâ, ârevisionistâ, and âpermanent revolutionâ are employed accurately and consistently.  Concepts like âbureaucratic patronageâ and âideological discourseâ are used correctly within historical context.

Minor language errors (e.g., âout manoeuvreâ, âKameneyâ) occasionally appear but do not obscure meaning or indicate conceptual confusion.

Overall precision and consistency place the essay within the 5-6 band; given the handful of spelling/grammar slips, 5 rather than 6 points are appropriate.

annotations.comment: The title is prominently placed and clearly identifies the essay as an Extended Essay in History, meeting formatting requirements.
Consider adding the course code or session (e.g., SL/HL) alongside the subject header to fully align with IB header conventions.
The research question is concise and sharply focused, effectively guiding the investigation of Leninâs testament in Stalinâs rise.
The word count is stated and falls within the 4000-word limit, demonstrating awareness of formal constraints.
The table of contents lists all required sections, aiding reader navigation and reflecting a clear structure.
Include page numbers next to each entry in the table of contents to enhance usability and conform with IB presentation guidelines.
The introduction effectively sets the historical context of Leninâs death and frames the ensuing power struggle.
The methodology rationale in the introduction is general; specify which archives, documents or analytical frameworks will be used to strengthen focus.
A web article from Counterfire is cited; its academic reliability is limited. Replace or critically evaluate such sources.
This section provides a balanced overview of traditional versus revisionist historiography, contextualizing the debate.
The historiographical discussion summarizes key positions but lacks interrogation of each historianâs biases; deepen critical evaluation.
Footnote markers appear with HTML artifacts (e.g., â<sup>â); apply a consistent, clean formatting style for citations.
The subheading clearly introduces the focused analysis of Leninâs Testament and its impact, maintaining coherence of structure.
When presenting primary extracts, assess their provenance and bias to strengthen source application rather than descriptive narration.
The exploration of Zinovievâs and Kamenevâs motives adds depth by revealing multi-causal dynamics in the suppression.
A student-level website is used as a source; evaluate its accuracy or substitute with peer-reviewed research.
The section on additional factors acknowledges complexity and supports a reasoned, multi-faceted argument.
When discussing appointments and party structures, specify dates or examples of personnel changes to bolster factual evidence.
There is a spelling error in referring to Trotsky (âTroskyâ); ensure all names and terms are accurate and consistent.
Further analyze how propaganda shifted perceptions within the party using additional contemporary examples beyond the funeral.
The heading clearly marks the start of the evaluation section, guiding the reader through the structure.
Consider introducing counterfactual scenarios when discussing long-term impact to deepen the critical evaluation.
The conclusion succinctly restates the central argument and integrates the main findings effectively.
Link the concluding remarks back to the historiographical debate introduced earlier to enhance coherence and scholarly dialogue.
The bibliography is neatly divided into primary and secondary sources, reflecting a systematic approach to research.
Citation styles vary across entries (e.g., italicization, punctuation); apply a uniform style guide for consistency.
When listing websites in the bibliography, annotate their selection criteria or credibility to demonstrate critical awareness of source reliability.
